section: Deformation behaviour of iron-rich iron-aluminum alloys at low temperatures

context_update.summary: This initial chunk presents the abstract, introduction, and experimental methodology of a study on the deformation behaviour of binary Fe-Al alloys (4–18 at.% Al) with low impurities (C < 100 wt.ppm). The research investigates the effects of quenching and annealing on as-cast polycrystalline and monocrystalline materials, tested at room temperature and −100 °C. The abstract reports that low-temperature stress-strain behaviour is primarily controlled by Al solid-solution hardening and quenched-in excess vacancies, with only minor short-range ordering effects. The introduction reviews related work and emphasises the K-state and ordering transitions (D0₃, B2) above ~10 at.% Al. The experimental section details alloy preparation (vacuum induction melting, high-purity Fe and Al, Cu-mould casting), monocrystal growth (Bridgman, 10–15 mm/h, ~12 h cycle), heat treatments (up to 1100 °C for 15 min; lower temperatures for days), specimen preparation, and mechanical testing procedures: microhardness (Fischerscope H100, 1 N, universal hardness HU), compression for 0.2% proof stress (5×5×10 mm³, 10⁻⁴ and 10⁻² s⁻¹), and tensile ductility (DIN 50125). Microstructural characterisation is outlined: optical microscopy, SEM/EDX, TEM (Philips CM20, 200 kV). Table 1 lists the studied alloys and impurities, which will be crucial for interpreting subsequent results.
processing_route: vacuum induction melting in Al2O3 crucibles
heat treatments: quenching from 1100 °C, annealing at lower temperatures, argon or evacuated SiO2 capsules
mechanisms: Al solid-solution hardening
quenched-in excess vacancies
short-range ordering effects
figures_or_tables: Table 1 (all alloys with compositions and impurities)

Key variables: Al content (4-18 at.%); material form (polycrystal vs monocrystal); heat treatment (quenching, annealing); testing temperature (room temperature, -100 °C); strain rate (10^-4 s^-1, 10^-2 s^-1); applied load (microhardness 1 N)

Properties: universal hardness HU; 0.2% compression proof stress; ductility (tensile)
Mechanisms: Al solid-solution hardening; quenched-in excess vacancies; short-range ordering effects

context_update.summary: This chunk reports microstructure, hardness, strength, ductility, and dislocation structures of binary Fe-Al alloys (4–18 at.% Al). Polycrystalline as-cast alloys have coarse grains (~1 mm) and no second phases; grain boundary carbides form after furnace cooling or 14-day 320 °C aging, with amount scaling with C content. Monocrystals contain rare AlN inclusions; carbides appear in Fe-17Al (70 ppm C) under 100 MPa load at 320 °C but not in Fe-16Al (45 ppm C). Microhardness of Fe-16Al (111) is insensitive to heat treatment (<10% variation). Compressive 0.2% proof stress increases linearly with Al (21–23 MPa/at.%), and twinning occurs in Fe-18Al. Tensile tests show increasing strength and decreasing ductility with Al; fracture is ductile at RT but increasingly brittle at −100 °C. Brittle fracture without necking occurs at 18% Al (RT) and >13% Al (−100 °C). Low-temperature strength is ~80 MPa higher. Yield drops appear in Fe-4Al and Fe-6Al at −100 °C. Ductility shows local maxima at 8 and 12% Al at RT. TEM reveals quenched Fe-12Al develops glide bands with tangles, furnace-cooled forms cells; quenched Fe-16Al has straight dislocations between glide bands, furnace-cooled yields tangles. These results set the stage for discussing short-range ordering and quenched-in vacancies.
material_systems: binary Fe-Al alloys (4-18 at.% Al, polycrystals)
microstructure: no second phases in as-cast state
grain boundary carbides after slow cooling or aging
AlN inclusions up to 30 µm in monocrystals
dislocation structures: glide bands, tangles, cell formation
mechanisms: Al solid-solution hardening (linear increase)
deformation twinning at high Al and low temperature
yield stress drop at -100 °C in low Al alloys
quenching effect on dislocation structure

context_update.summary: This chunk first presents TEM observations of dislocation structures after room-temperature compression in two polycrystalline alloys. In Fe-12%Al (60 ppm C), quenching leads to glide bands with dislocation tangles while furnace cooling promotes cell formation; before deformation, the quenched state contains only few straight dislocations and the furnace-cooled state shows almost none. In Fe-16%Al (<20 ppm C), quenching produces multi-directional glide bands with straight dislocations between them, whereas furnace cooling yields tangles between glide bands. The discussion then addresses short-range ordering: it occurs in 13–18% Al alloys below 600 °C, is maximal after 250 °C/14 days (Fe-18%Al) with an activation energy of 0.3 eV, and is suppressed by 1100 °C quenching. However, hardness measurements on Fe-16%Al monocrystals demonstrate that short-range order has no significant effect on strength; only heat treatments above 600 °C alter the mechanical response. The dominant factor is quenched-in excess vacancies: their formation enthalpy (1–1.2 eV for 16–18% Al) leads to high concentrations that increase with Al content, pin dislocations, and produce the highest hardness after quenching. Healing of excess vacancies is slow and may be incomplete even after 300 °C/14 day anneals. Comparisons with Fe-40%Al and earlier literature reinforce the vacancy hardening mechanism. This analysis sets the foundation for interpreting strength and ductility trends in terms of vacancy effects rather than ordering.
processing_route: annealing at 300 °C/6 h and 3 days and 14 days
mechanisms: short-range ordering: minor or no effect on hardness and strength in Fe-Al up to 18% Al
quenched-in excess vacancy hardening: vacancies pin dislocations, increase hardness and yield stress
vacancy healing: slow kinetics, incomplete below 400 °C
vacancy-promoted fracture along slip planes in high-Al alloys

context_update.summary: Section 4.3 reveals that the yield stress of polycrystalline Fe-Al alloys up to 18 at.% Al increases linearly with Al content, described by Suzuki’s solid-solution hardening theory, with a hardening coefficient of ~2.2 GPa (insensitive to strain rate and temperature). Deviations at −100 °C are attributed to deformation twinning. The elongation before necking also decreases linearly with Al content, while the fracture strain steeply drops beyond 15% Al (RT) and 12% Al (−100 °C), reaching coincidence with elongation at 18% Al (RT) and 14% Al (−100 °C), indicating a ductile-to-brittle transition. TEM (Figs 6–7) shows that only furnace-cooled Fe-12%Al develops dislocation cell structures, confirming reduced dislocation mobility in higher-Al alloys; quenching further reduces mobility via excess vacancies. Section 4.4 reports yield stress drops exclusively in Fe-4%Al and Fe-6%Al at −100 °C. Deformation twinning (with audible clicks) occurs at RT only for Fe-18%Al, but at −100 °C for 11–18% Al. Both phenomena are linked to reduced mobile dislocation density with increasing Al content and decreasing temperature. Excess vacancies may contribute to the yield drops, but further study is needed. Key figures: Fig. 3, 4(b), 5(a,b), 6, 7.
processing_route: quenching (referenced in relation to excess vacancies)
microstructure: cell formation in furnace-cooled Fe-12%Al after deformation
properties: yield stress linear increase with Al
fracture strain transition at 18%Al RT and 14%Al -100°C
yield stress drops in Fe-4%Al and Fe-6%Al at -100°C
mechanisms: solid-solution hardening (Suzuki theory)
quenched-in excess vacancies reduce dislocation mobility
reduced screw dislocation mobility prerequisite for twinning
excess vacancies may cause yield drops in low-Al alloys
decreased dislocation mobility leads to brittle fracture
unresolved_terms: precise role of excess vacancies in yield drops needs further study
discrepancy with Davies' 25% yield stress increase after 300°C anneal

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the last part of Section 4.4 on deformation twinning and the complete Conclusions (Section 5). Section 4.4 states that at room temperature only Fe-18%Al exhibits deformation twinning, evidenced by loud clicks and load drops in Fig. 4(b) and metallographic observations (Figs. 3, 4(b), 5(b)). At −100 °C deformation twinning occurs for Al contents from 11% to 18%. The mechanism involves insufficient active dislocation glide systems and reduced screw dislocation mobility, which is enhanced by substitutional alloying and decreasing temperature. The Conclusions summarize the main findings of the entire study: short-range ordering controlled below 600 °C does not significantly affect deformation, while treatments above 600 °C do; quenching introduces hardening excess vacancies that anneal out slowly; yield stress increases linearly with Al up to 18 at.% at RT following Suzuki's theory; ductility decreases with Al, with a ductile-to-brittle transition at ~15% Al (RT) and ~12% Al (−100 °C); low-temperature yield drops result from dislocation-vacancy interactions; and twinning is promoted by lower temperature and higher Al due to reduced dislocation mobility. This chunk provides the final mechanistic framework and property trends that underpin the paper’s conclusions.
material_systems: binary Fe-Al alloys (up to 18 at.% Al)
key_variables: strain rate (implied but not specified in this chunk)
properties: ductility (plastic elongation before necking, fracture strain)
mechanisms: solid-solution hardening (Suzuki's theory)
excess vacancy hardening
dislocation-vacancy interaction causing yield drops

At −100 °C deformation twinning occurs for Al contents from 11% to 18%. The mechanism involves insufficient active dislocation glide systems and reduced screw dislocation mobility, which is enhanced by substitutional alloying and decreasing temperature. The Conclusions summarize the main findings of the entire study: short-range ordering controlled below 600 °C does not significantly affect deformation, while treatments above 600 °C do; quenching introduces hardening excess vacancies that anneal out slowly; yield stress increases linearly with Al up to 18 at.% at RT following Suzuki's theory; ductility decreases with Al, with a ductile-to-brittle transition at ~15% Al (RT) and ~12% Al (−100 °C); low-temperature yield drops result from dislocation-vacancy interactions; and twinning is promoted by lower temperature and higher Al due to reduced dislocation mobility. This chunk provides the final mechanistic framework and property trends that underpin the paper’s conclusions.
Material systems: binary Fe-Al alloys (4-18 at.% Al); binary Fe-Al alloys (4-18 at.% Al, polycrystals); Fe-16%Al monocrystal; Fe-17%Al monocrystal; Fe-12%Al polycrystal (60 ppm C); Fe-16%Al polycrystal (<20 ppm C); Fe-18%Al; Fe-40%Al (B2)
Sample aliases: Fe-16% monocrystal; Fe-17% monocrystal; Fe-4Al polycrystal; Fe-6Al polycrystal; Fe-18Al polycrystal; Fe-12Al polycrystal; Fe-16Al polycrystal (low C); Fe-12%Al (60 ppm C) polycrystal
Processing route: vacuum induction melting in Al2O3 crucibles; Cu-mould casting (rods ~18 mm diameter); Bridgman monocrystal growth (10-15 mm/h); heat treatments: quenching from 1100 °C, annealing at lower temperatures, argon or evacuated SiO2 capsules; electrostatic discharge machining; mechanical polishing; ultrasonic cleaning in acetone; as-cast
Key variables: Al content (4-18 at.%); material form (polycrystal vs monocrystal); heat treatment (quenching, annealing); testing temperature (room temperature, -100 °C); strain rate (10^-4 s^-1, 10^-2 s^-1); applied load (microhardness 1 N); C content (20-80 wt.ppm); testing temperature (RT, -100 °C)
Microstructure: K-state (short-range ordering); D0₃; B2; coarse grains ~1 mm in polycrystals; no second phases in as-cast state; grain boundary carbides after slow cooling or aging; AlN inclusions up to 30 µm in monocrystals; dislocation structures: glide bands, tangles, cell formation
Properties: universal hardness HU; 0.2% compression proof stress; ductility (tensile); 0.2% compressive proof stress; tensile yield stress; ultimate tensile strength; fracture strain; uniform strain (strain before necking)
